medications.indication_rationale: Treats the candidal (fungal) load while the low-potency corticosteroid rapidly settles the intense inflammatory burning. Combined antifungal-plus-steroid is appropriate for the acutely inflamed, symptomatic phase; it must be prescribed and applied under in-person review, not begun blindly via telehealth.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically to the affected skin folds (source note did not state an application frequency — do not synthesise one)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid prolonged or potent topical corticosteroid on macerated, fissured or potentially bacterially-infected skin — keep the steroid component low-potency and short-term

medications.indication_rationale: A short oral antifungal course is warranted given the extensive surface area and the depth of the fissures, which exceed what topical therapy alone reliably clears. Prescribed by the in-person reviewer.
medications.dose_route_frequency: oral fluconazole, 7-day course (source note stated a 7-day course but no milligram dose — dose NOT synthesised; the prescriber sets the dose)

medications.indication_rationale: Applied as the second step after the antifungal has absorbed, a thick zinc barrier cream blocks urine and moisture from contacting the raw fissured skin, allowing it to heal and preventing recurrence.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thick layer over the folds after the antifungal absorbs, at every pad change

medications.drug_name: Empiric oral antibiotics (for the skin) without confirmed secondary bacterial infection
medications.indication_rationale: Adding empiric antibiotics is an error of commission here: broad-spectrum antibiotics are the driver of the candidal overgrowth, and giving more would worsen it. Antibiotics are warranted only if genuine secondary bacterial cellulitis is confirmed on in-person examination.

Corticosteroid alone suppresses the inflammation while allowing the yeast to proliferate (candida/tinea incognito) and can thin fragile fissured skin. Any steroid must be low-potency, short-term and combined with an antifungal.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: To monitor the deep bleeding fissures, guide dressing/moisture management and detect early secondary infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict two-step routine at every pad change: apply the medicated antifungal cream first, let it absorb, then apply a thick zinc barrier cream over it.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Sequencing the antifungal then the barrier both treats the yeast and shields the raw skin from urine, which is essential for the fissures to heal.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Air-dry the folds by placing rolled dry towels under the skin folds for about 30 minutes twice a day and keep the folds meticulously dry between cares.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing moisture and occlusion removes the environment the yeast needs to grow and prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Continue the repositioning/turning schedule (with adequate analgesia so pain does not prevent it) and use a high-absorbency, breathable incontinence pad system; keep the room cooler to reduce sweating.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintaining turns prevents pressure injury, and reducing moisture, heat and friction addresses the perpetuating factors.

patient_education_points.point: The recent antibiotics reduced the normal skin bacteria and let a yeast overgrow in the warm, moist folds. The oral tablet works from the inside to clear the yeast, and the cream calms the severe burning and treats the area.
Keep the skin folds meticulously dry, air-dry them twice a day, and always apply the medicated cream first (let it absorb) before the zinc barrier cream.
These infections in deep folds come back easily if moisture is not controlled — the drying and barrier routine has to continue even after it looks better.
The burning should ease within about 24 hours with the cream; the fissures usually take 7–10 days to close with strict dryness and the oral antifungal.
Nursing staff should watch for spreading redness, a fever, hard swelling under the skin, or pain worsening despite the creams, and seek urgent review if any occur.

follow_up_plan.interval: same-day in-person medical review to start treatment, then GP/aged-care review in about 3 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: that the fissures are granulating and the erythema is receding, that no secondary cellulitis has developed, and that pain is controlled enough to allow turning and hygiene
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: pain worsening despite the new creams, spreading redness, new fever, hard swelling under the skin, or any black/necrotic patches

must_include_items: Recognise the picture as candidal intertrigo of the skin folds with maceration/fissuring and secondary-infection risk
Arrange a SAME-DAY in-person medical examination of the skin folds (to inspect fissure depth and exclude secondary bacterial cellulitis) rather than managing entirely by telehealth
Direct antifungal-based treatment (topical antifungal +/- short-term low-potency steroid, plus an oral antifungal for the extensive/deep disease) prescribed by the in-person reviewer
Identify the recent broad-spectrum antibiotic course as the driver of the candidal overgrowth
Institute strict moisture control — air-drying and the antifungal-then-barrier-cream two-step at every pad change
Hand over conservative safety-netting to nursing staff for spreading infection / sepsis, with the specific triggers
acceptable_alternatives: Nystatin, miconazole or another topical antifungal instead of clotrimazole
If a same-day GP/locum home visit cannot be secured, an urgent-care or after-hours home-visit service the same day is acceptable
Deferring the oral antifungal to the in-person reviewer's judgement, provided same-day review and topical antifungal + moisture control are arranged
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person examination and excluding secondary bacterial cellulitis
Failing to give conservative safety-netting for spreading infection or sepsis
Not addressing the moisture/occlusion that perpetuates the condition
Treating with a corticosteroid alone without an antifungal
errors_of_commission: Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics as the treatment when there is no confirmed secondary bacterial infection
Escalating to an ambulance/000 or ED transfer when there is no systemic sepsis and no necrotising features (over-triage of a same-day in-person problem)
Attempting full remote management and reassurance without any in-person examination
Inventing a specific drug dose not stated in the source
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) arranging same-day in-person review with examination to exclude secondary bacterial cellulitis, (b) antifungal-based treatment with moisture control, and (c) conservative safety-netting for deterioration. Managing as self-care only is a critical under-triage failure; escalating straight to 000/ED with no systemic sepsis is an over-triage error.
